sanskrit_devanagari:
अर्जुन उवाच
किं तद्ब्रह्म किमध्यात्मं किं कर्म पुरुषोत्तम ।
अधिभूतं च किं प्रोक्तमधिदैवं किमुच्यते ॥ १ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
arjuna uvāca
kiṁ tad brahma kim adhyātmaṁ kiṁ karma puruṣottama |
adhibhūtaṁ ca kiṁ proktam adhidaivaṁ kim ucyate || 1 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence identical).
exact (transliteration body identical; speaker tag arjuna uvāca on its own line).
exact (text body identical; verse-number marker rendered as ।।8.1।।)
exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_08.001 = MBh_06,030.001). Body text identical.

anvaya.meaning: is called / is said to be

translations.verbatim_quote: Arjuna inquired: O my Lord, O Supreme Person, what is Brahman? What is the self? What are fruitive activities? What is this material manifestation? And what are the demigods? Please explain this to me.
Arjun said: O Supreme Lord, what is Brahman (Absolute Reality), what is adhyatma (the individual soul), and what is karma? What is said to be adhibhuta, and who is said to be adhidaiv?

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: In this chapter Lord Kṛṣṇa answers different questions from Arjuna, beginning with "What is Brahman?" The Lord also explains karma (fruitive activities), devotional service and yoga principles, and devotional service in its pure form.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 8.1– 8.2 Arjuna said — What are that brahman, Adhyatma and Karma which have been mentioned as what should be known by those who aspire for release from old age and death while they take refuge with the Lord? What are Adhibuta and Adhidaiva, which should be known by the aspirants for wealth?
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja groups 8.1-8.2 together and reads Arjuna's seven questions as scoped to three classes of aspirant: (a) those seeking release from old-age-and-death and taking refuge in the Lord need to know brahman, adhyātma, karma; (b) those seeking wealth need to know adhibhūta and adhidaiva; (c) all three groups need to know the adhiyajña and the manner of knowing-the-Lord at the time of departure. The questions are doctrinally structured, not casual.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara explicitly defers his commentary on 8.1 to 8.2 — the seven questions are treated as a single doctrinal unit. The bhasya proper appears at 8.2, where Shankara addresses all seven questions together as the chapter's structural opening.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Hari OM! The previous six chapters known as the section on karma yoga or the science of the individual consciousness attaining communion with the ultimate consciousness by selfless actions, the method of acquiring spiritual wisdom was described. Now the next six chapters known as the bhakti yoga section or the yoga of devotion primarily describe the glories and greatness of the Supreme Lord.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Madhva sets the structural frame: Gita chapters 1-6 are the karma-yoga section, chapters 7-12 are the bhakti-yoga section, chapters 13-18 are the jñāna-yoga section. Chapter 8 sits in the bhakti-yoga middle section. Arjuna's seven questions open the doctrinally structured exposition of the Lord's glories.

disagreements_among_translators.word: the seven questions (brahma / adhyātma / karma / puruṣottama / adhibhūta / adhidaiva — and the implied seventh from 8.2)

positions.rendering: five questions in 8.1 (Brahman, the self, fruitive activities, material manifestation, demigods) plus two in 8.2 (Lord-of-sacrifice, knowing-at-time-of-death)
five questions in 8.1 (Brahman/Absolute Reality, adhyātma/individual soul, karma, adhibhūta, adhidaiv) plus two in 8.2
the seven questions are a single doctrinal unit, treated together at 8.2
the seven questions are scoped to three classes of aspirant: release-seekers, wealth-seekers, and all-three at the time of death
nine questions raised, not seven (counting kim and katham distinctly)

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: The verse opens with five technical Sanskrit terms — brahma, adhyātma, karma, adhibhūta, adhidaiva — and a vocative (puruṣottama). 8.2 adds adhiyajña and the manner-of-knowing-at-time-of-death, completing the canonical 'seven questions' count. Translators agree on the surface count (five-then-two = seven) but diverge on the structural reading: Shankara treats them as a single doctrinal unit; Ramanuja scopes them by class of aspirant; Abhinavagupta counts them differently (nine, distinguishing kim from katham). The Sanskrit technical terms (adhyātma, adhibhūta, adhidaiva, adhiyajña) are doctrinally specific and resist flat English equivalents — this is the chapter's central translation difficulty and the basis for the engineering layer's STRETCHED tagging.

positions.rendering: vocative; addressed to Krishna as the Supreme Person
vocative addressed to the Lord as Supreme Person

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: puruṣottama is a Krishna-specific epithet that becomes the title of chapter 15 (Puruṣottama-Yoga). All translators render it consistently here as 'Supreme Person' or 'Supreme Lord'. The vocative force frames Arjuna's questions: he is asking the Supreme Person directly, naming him as such, before posing the technical questions about Brahman / adhyātma etc.

literal_meaning: Arjuna said: What is that Brahman? What is adhyātma? What is karma, O Supreme Person? What is called adhibhūta, and what is said to be adhidaiva?
traditional_meaning_consensus: Arjuna opens chapter 8 with five of the seven canonical questions that structure the chapter (the remaining two — adhiyajña and the manner-of-knowing-at-time-of-death — follow immediately at 8.2). The terms are Sanskrit-technical: brahma (the imperishable supreme), adhyātma (the self / one's own nature, svabhāva), karma (the offering causing origination of beings), adhibhūta (the perishable existence), adhidaiva (the puruṣa / divine principle). Each will be defined precisely by Krishna at 8.3-8.4. The opening verse establishes the chapter's structure: Arjuna asks crisply, Krishna defines crisply. Shankara defers his commentary to 8.2 because the seven questions form a single doctrinal unit. Ramanuja groups the questions by class of aspirant. The verse establishes the chapter's exposition-at-cosmic-register voice — questions about the substrate of the substrate.

remaining_gaps: Shankara verbatim deferred to 8.2 by Shankara himself; not a scrape gap.
